tasks.title: AFL Fuzzing Series
tasks.summary: A chain of 5 papers advancing coverage-based greybox fuzzing. AFL establishes the core coverage-guided fuzzing algorithm. AFLFast models fuzzing as a Markov chain and introduces power schedules to bias exploration towards low-frequency paths. AFLGo adds directed fuzzing via distance computation and simulated annealing. FairFuzz targets rare branches through mutation masking. AFL++ combines these advances into a modular extensible framework with custom mutator API and MOpt mutation scheduling.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a series of 5 coverage-guided greybox fuzzing algorithms as a Python simulation framework. Working directory: /workspace. The base `fuzzsim/` package provides program model, coverage bitmap, mutation operators, and simulated target programs. Implement: (1) AFL core fuzzer with full deterministic stages (bitflip/arith/interest/dict), havoc, splice, queue management with calibration and trimming, corpus culling via greedy set cover, crash deduplication, fork server simulation, and constant power schedule; (2) AFLFast Markov chain power schedules (FAST, COE, EXPLORE, EXPLOIT, LINEAR, QUAD) with path frequency tracking, state transition modeling, and seed prioritization strategies; (3) AFLGo directed fuzzing with call-graph and CFG distance computation (harmonic mean), distance normalization, simulated annealing with multiple cooling schedules (exponential/linear/logarithmic/adaptive), and distance-aware seed prioritization; (4) FairFuzz rare branch targeting with dynamic threshold computation, probe-based influence detection, mutation mask generation with caching, and targeted havoc preserving influential bytes; (5) AFL++ custom mutator API (init/fuzz/havoc_mutation/ post_process/trim/queue_new_entry), MutatorRegistry with dynamic weights, GrammarMutator, CmpLog/RedQueen mutator, MOpt PSO-based mutation scheduling (pilot/core phases with velocity/position updates), and PowerScheduleEnsemble rotation. Each module's run.sh section outputs EXPERIMENT_NAME: VALUE lines. Tests verify deterministic results with seed=42.

tasks.title: Alloy Formal Modeling Tool Chain
tasks.summary: A chain of formal modeling tools reimplemented in Python from scratch based on the research papers. Starting with Alloy's specification language and relational logic (Jackson, 2002), through Kodkod's SAT-based model finding engine with symmetry breaking (Torlak & Jackson, 2007), Pardinus's temporal logic extensions with decomposed solving (Macedo et al., 2016), to Electrum's full temporal specification analyzer with trace animation (Macedo et al., 2018). Each module builds incrementally on the previous, forming a complete formal verification pipeline.

tasks.title: angr Concolic Execution Pipeline
tasks.summary: A five-module chain in the concolic/symbolic execution domain built on the angr binary analysis framework. The first module establishes core binary analysis capabilities (CFG recovery, function discovery, symbolic path exploration). The second module implements Driller, which uses selective symbolic execution to augment fuzzing by generating inputs that cross compartment boundaries guarded by magic number checks. The third module implements Cozy, a comparative symbolic execution engine that analyzes two binary versions (pre-patch and post-patch) to identify behavioral differences and generate concrete witness inputs. The fourth module implements forward taint propagation to track user-controlled data flow through binaries and identify when tainted data reaches dangerous sinks. The fifth module implements symbolic vulnerability pattern detection combining taint analysis with constraint solving to detect buffer overflows, format strings, and use-after-free.

tasks.title: CDCL SAT Solver Evolution: MiniSAT to Glucose
tasks.summary: A two-paper evolution chain implementing CDCL (Conflict-Driven Clause Learning) SAT solvers. Paper 1 implements MiniSAT, a minimalist but complete CDCL solver with two-watched-literal BCP, first-UIP conflict analysis, clause minimization, VSIDS decision heuristic, and activity-based clause database reduction. Paper 2 implements Glucose, which extends MiniSAT with LBD (Literal Block Distance) based clause quality assessment, dynamic LBD updates, LBD-based restart strategies, LBD-based database reduction, and Learnt Clause Minimization (LCM). Uses a directory-copy pattern where Glucose starts from a copy of MiniSAT.
tasks.instructionPreview: **Overview**: This task covers the evolution of CDCL (Conflict-Driven Clause Learning) SAT solvers, from the foundational MiniSAT solver to Glucose which extends it with LBD-based learnt clause management. You will implement the core CDCL algorithm in MiniSAT, then extend it in Glucose with improved clause quality heuristics based on the Literal Block Distance (LBD) metric.

tasks.title: CMM Compiler (Lexer + Parser + Semantics + IR + MIPS)
tasks.summary: A multi-stage compiler for CMM (a C-like language) that produces MIPS assembly. The pipeline proceeds through lexical analysis (flex), syntax analysis (bison), semantic analysis, three-address intermediate code (IR) generation, and MIPS assembly emission. The lexer, parser, AST, symbol table, and IR data structures are pre-existing; the three implementation stages—semantic analysis, IR generation, and MIPS generation—must be built in pipeline order since each stage consumes outputs of the previous one.
tasks.instructionPreview: This experiment implements a multi-stage CMM compiler for an undergraduate compilers course (a C-like language) that produces MIPS assembly. Several core functions have been removed and must be re-implemented.

tasks.title: CVC SMT Solver Family
tasks.summary: A chain of three papers describing the evolution of the CVC family of SMT solvers. CVC4 adds competition-grade theory solvers (bit-vectors, non-linear arithmetic, arrays) with a fully rewritten SAT core (2WL, CDCL, restarts) and Nelson-Oppen theory combination. CVC4Sy extends CVC4 with syntax-guided synthesis (SyGuS) capabilities including CEGIS, grammar normalization, and multi-function synthesis support. cvc5 introduces proof production (Alethe format), theories of finite sets and sequences, quantifier instantiation (MBQI), incremental solving, and optimization.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a simplified SMT solver in Python that evolves through three stages, corresponding to three papers in the CVC solver family.

tasks.title: DCQCN RDMA Congestion Control Evolution Chain

tasks.title: EvoSuite Test Generation Series
tasks.summary: A chain of 6 modules advancing search-based test generation. EvoSuite establishes whole test suite optimization with a genetic algorithm. MOSA reformulates branch coverage as a many-objective problem with Pareto non-dominated sorting. The Whole-Suite Archive enhances GA with per-goal archiving and secondary objectives. DynaMOSA introduces dynamic many-objective sorting with preference criterion and control dependency graph. aDynaMOSA adds performance-aware test generation with adaptive heuristic switching. EMOTE implements modular testing with relaxed prefix constraints and selective coverage contribution.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a series of 6 search-based test generation algorithms as a Python framework. Working directory: /workspace. The base `src/` package provides program model (CFG with branches), test case representation, execution engine, genetic operators, and 5 benchmark programs. Implement: (1) EvoSuite whole test suite GA with branch coverage fitness; (2) MOSA many-objective sorting algorithm treating each branch as a separate objective with Pareto non-dominated sorting, preference criterion, and crowding distance; (3) Whole-Suite Archive GA with per-goal archiving, secondary objectives (test length, exception diversity, mutation score), and post-search minimization pass; (4) DynaMOSA with preference-based sorting, coverage archive, and dynamic target selection via control dependency graph; (5) aDynaMOSA with 7 performance proxies, stagnation detection, and adaptive heuristic switching; (6) EMOTE modular testing with relaxed prefix constraints and selective coverage contribution. Each module's experiments/run_all.py outputs ALGORITHM_METRIC: VALUE lines. Tests verify deterministic results with seed=42.

Both papers evolve the same C++ codebase in place.

tasks.title: ns-3 Network Simulation Series — LTE and mmWave Modules
tasks.summary: A series of network simulation papers building on the ns-3 discrete-event network simulator. The first paper introduces the LTE-LENA module, providing complete 4G LTE cellular network simulation within ns-3, including eNodeB/UE devices, EPC (Evolved Packet Core), MAC schedulers, and multi-layer protocol stack (PHY, MAC, RLC, PDCP, RRC). The second paper extends the LTE module with a 5G millimeter wave (mmWave) simulation module, adding customizable TDD frame structures, mmWave channel models (3-state propagation, MIMO beamforming), and mmWave-specific PHY and MAC layers for high-frequency cellular communication research.
tasks.instructionPreview: This task involves implementing two network simulation modules for the ns-3 discrete-event network simulator. The workspace contains the ns-3 C++ framework with core simulation engine, network stack, mobility models, spectrum/propagation modules, and build system (CMake).

tasks.title: Operating Systems Labs 2-4 (x86 kernel)
tasks.summary: A four-lab OS course project where each lab is a self-contained bootable x86 kernel. Lab 1 (bootloader) is pre-implemented; Labs 2-4 add system calls, process management, and process synchronization respectively. Each lab shares the same three-subsystem structure: VGA framebuffer routines, kernel IRQ/syscall handlers, and a user-space I/O library.
tasks.instructionPreview: This is a four-lab undergraduate operating-systems course project. Each lab builds a bootable x86 OS image from C and assembly source. The labs progress from bootloader through system calls, process management, and process synchronization. Lab 1 is already complete; you must implement Labs 2–4.

tasks.title: Operating Systems — x86 ASM/Kernel Labs 2–8
tasks.summary: A comprehensive x86 32-bit OS implementation course spanning Labs 2–8. Students implement assembly routines, C/C++/ASM interop, kernel utilities (stdlib, linked list, bitmap, address pool), and kernel subsystems (VGA stdio, interrupt management, round-robin thread scheduling, synchronization primitives, and virtual memory management). Each module's implementation is replicated across up to 19 lab exercise snapshots; the same logic must be filled in for every copy.
tasks.instructionPreview: This is a comprehensive task from an undergraduate operating-systems course. The project spans Labs 2–8 and covers x86 assembly programming, OS utility data structures, kernel I/O, interrupt handling, thread management, synchronization primitives, and memory management. You must implement all stubbed-out functions (marked with `/* TODO: implement */` or bare assembly labels) across the entire codebase so that all tests pass.

tasks.title: OS & Distributed Systems Lab Assignments
tasks.summary: Lab practicals covering OS fundamentals (CPU scheduling, memory placement, page replacement) in Java and distributed systems concepts (Lamport logical clocks, ring/bully election algorithms) in Python. Each module is a standalone lab exercise with no code dependencies between modules; all five groups can be implemented in parallel.
tasks.instructionPreview: This repository contains implementations of systems programming and distributed systems practicals from an undergraduate computer-engineering laboratory sequence. The code covers CPU scheduling algorithms, memory placement strategies, page replacement policies (Java), and distributed systems concepts including Lamport logical clocks and election algorithms (Python). Core algorithm implementations have been removed and must be re-implemented.

tasks.title: OS Course Labs (Projects 1–6, RISC-V Bare-Metal OS)
tasks.summary: An operating systems lab that incrementally builds a bare-metal OS on a dual-core RV64IMAFDC SiFive Rocket processor across six projects (seven directories). Starts with a host-side bootloader image tool and a cooperative kernel, then evolves through preemptive scheduling with synchronisation primitives, process management with IPC, virtual memory with demand paging, a network device driver, and a full on-disk file system. Each later project directory carries forward and extends the scheduler and memory subsystems of the previous one.
tasks.instructionPreview: This is an operating-systems course lab (Projects 1–6) that implements a RISC-V bare-metal operating system. The repo spans six incremental projects: a bootloader, a simple cooperative kernel, a preemptive kernel with SMP, an interactive OS with process management and IPC, a virtual-memory-enabled kernel with demand paging, a device-driver layer with network support, and a file system. Entire subsystems have been removed and must be re-implemented.

tasks.title: Panda3D Collision Arena Hard
tasks.summary: A two-phase bootstrap task: first repair the intentionally hollowed Panda3D C++ collision subsystem (CollisionTraverser, CollisionSphere, CollisionBox, CollisionCapsule, CollisionPlane, CollisionInvSphere, CollisionLevelStateBase, CollisionHandlerQueue, CollisionHandlerPusher), then implement the headless Python game scripts that exercise the repaired engine via a deterministic multi-type collision replay. The game records collision events and a SHA-256-verified final state used for correctness checks.
tasks.instructionPreview: You are given: - A Panda3D engine source tree at /workspace/panda3d - A deterministic headless mini-game project at /workspace/game_project (CollisionArenaHard) - Replay and expected-output assets under /workspace/assets - Helper scripts under /workspace/scripts

tasks.instructionPreview: You are given: - A Panda3D engine source tree at /workspace/panda3d - A deterministic headless mini-game project at /workspace/game_project (SceneGraphArenaHard) - Replay and expected-output assets under /workspace/assets - Helper scripts under /workspace/scripts

tasks.title: Perses Program Reduction — Syntax-Guided Reduction Framework
tasks.summary: A Python-based implementation of syntax-guided program reduction techniques from the Perses family of papers. The framework uses SparTree (syntax-aware parse tree) representation with PNF-normalized grammars to reduce programs while preserving a target property. Five papers are implemented: Perses (base syntax-guided reducer), Vulcan (1-minimality extensions), PPR (pairwise program reduction), LPR (LLM-aided reduction), and Sivand (neural code model interpretability via program reduction). Only Python 3 standard library is required; no external dependencies.
tasks.instructionPreview: This project is a Python-based program reduction framework implementing techniques from the Perses family of research papers. Program reduction takes a program that triggers a bug (or exhibits some property) and produces a minimal program that still triggers the same bug.

tasks.title: Query Optimization Polystore Framework
tasks.summary: A query optimization framework inspired by three foundational papers in database systems. Apache Calcite introduces a modular, extensible query processing framework with a Volcano-style cost-based optimizer, relational algebra, SQL parsing, and an adapter architecture for heterogeneous data sources. Polypheny-DB extends this with a polystore model combining multiple data store types (relational, document, key-value), cost-based query routing, data partitioning, and adaptive data placement. Orca (GPORCA) adds a distributed MPP query optimizer with DXL intermediate representation, memo-based plan enumeration, transformation/implementation rules, and distributed cost modeling.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a query optimization, polystore, and distributed MPP optimizer framework in Python. The project has three modules to implement in order:

tasks.title: RDMA ns-3 Simulator Evolution Chain
tasks.summary: A three-paper evolution chain of ns-3 RDMA network simulators sharing the QBB (Queue-Building-Block) transport framework. Paper 1 (HPCC) implements INT-based high-precision congestion control algorithms. Paper 2 (TLT-RDMA) upgrades the ns-3 base from 3.18 to 3.19 with timeout-less transport extensions. Paper 3 (ConWeave) adds congestion-aware in-network load balancing with packet reordering support. Uses a sequential directory copy pattern where each paper builds atop the previous one.
tasks.instructionPreview: **Overview**: This task covers a three-paper evolution chain of ns-3 RDMA network simulators, all sharing the same QBB (Queue-Building-Block) RDMA transport framework. You will implement each paper's congestion-control and load-balancing contributions in its own directory, progressively building from HPCC's INT-based high-precision rate control to TLT-RDMA's timeout-less transport extensions, and finally ConWeave's in-network reordering and load-balanced multipath RDMA.

tasks.title: RISC-V 64-bit CPU — SystemVerilog Pipelined Design
tasks.summary: A RV64IM pipelined CPU implemented in SystemVerilog and simulated with Verilator. The design has a classic 5-stage pipeline (Fetch/Decode/Execute/Memory/Writeback), a 2-way set-associative data cache backed by a CBus-to-AXI bridge, and CSR/hazard support for exceptions, interrupts, and multi-cycle multiply/divide. Only modules containing TODO stubs (19 patched files) are represented here.
tasks.instructionPreview: This project is a RISC-V 64-bit CPU (FDCPU teaching core) implemented in SystemVerilog, simulated using Verilator. The CPU features a 5-stage pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Writeback), a set-associative data cache (DCache), hazard detection with forwarding and stalling, CSR (Control and Status Register) support, and a CBus-to-AXI bus bridge.

tasks.title: Scotty3D — Computer Graphics Application
tasks.summary: Scotty3D is a software 3D graphics application. This task requires implementing scene-graph transform composition (Assignment 1) and halfedge mesh editing operations (Assignment 2): local operations that rewire individual edges and faces, and global operations that reprocess the entire mesh topology. Three files are patched: src/scene/transform.cpp, src/geometry/halfedge-local.cpp, and src/geometry/halfedge-global.cpp.
tasks.instructionPreview: This is the Scotty3D 3D graphics teaching codebase. The project implements a software rasterizer, path tracer, and half-edge mesh editor. Multiple core modules have been left unimplemented (returning stubs) and must be filled in so that all tests pass.

tasks.title: Security Labs

tasks.instructionPreview: This task is based on an undergraduate computer-security lab sequence. You will implement a set of security-related units covering encoding, serialization, cryptographic attacks, Merkle-tree proofs, Python escape attacks, and a WebAssembly sandbox wrapper.

tasks.summary: A three-paper chain implementing a Java static analysis framework. Soot provides the intermediate representation and call graph construction. FlowDroid builds IFDS-based taint analysis for detecting data leaks. StubDroid adds automatic library summary generation for performance.

tasks.title: Stream Mining / Hoeffding Trees
tasks.summary: A progressive implementation of data stream mining algorithms, starting from the foundational VFDT (Hoeffding Tree) for learning from stationary high-speed streams, extending to CVFDT for handling concept drift in time-changing streams, adding ADWIN for adaptive windowing drift detection, culminating in the MOA framework with ensemble methods, and SAM-KNN for self-adjusting memory-based classification under heterogeneous concept drift.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a data stream mining framework in Python. The workspace contains base components: stream generators (RandomTreeGenerator, RotatingHyperplaneGenerator, WaveformGenerator), evaluation utilities (prequential evaluation), base classifier classes, and attribute observers.

tasks.title: Systems Programming Labs (Four Modules)
tasks.summary: Four independent lab assignments covering core systems topics: a segregated-free-list memory allocator (C), two bash scripting utilities, a UNIX shell with piping and signal handling (C++), and a multi-threaded HTTP server with CGI and directory listing (C++). Each lab is independent at the cross-lab level but has an internal implementation order within its own files.

tasks.title: SysY to Koopa IR / RISC-V Compiler (Rust)
tasks.summary: A compiler for the SysY language (C subset) that translates source code first into Koopa IR (frontend) and then into RISC-V assembly (backend). The frontend consists of an expression builder, a statement builder, and a declaration builder feeding a top-level compilation-unit entry point; the backend pairs an LRU register allocator with an instruction-level code generator. AST definitions and the LALRPOP parser are pre-existing and require no implementation.
tasks.instructionPreview: This project is an undergraduate compiler for the SysY language (a C subset) that translates source code into Koopa IR and RISC-V assembly. The compiler is written in Rust and uses the `lalrpop` parser generator along with the `koopa` IR library. It supports expressions (arithmetic, logical with short-circuit evaluation), control flow (if/else, while, break, continue), functions (definitions, calls, parameters), variable/constant declarations, and arrays (including multi-dimensional).

tasks.title: TCP-Like Reliable Transport over Simulated UDP
tasks.summary: A computer networking course project that builds a fully functional TCP-like reliable transport protocol on top of a simulated UDP network. The project covers packet framing, connection setup via a three-way handshake, reliable data transfer with sliding-window flow control, RTT estimation, retransmission, and TCP-style congestion control. Two files require implementation: the packet serialization layer (tju_packet.c) and the TCP protocol engine (tju_tcp.c).
tasks.instructionPreview: This project implements a TCP-like reliable transport protocol (course teaching stack) over a simulated UDP network. A lightweight kernel layer receives UDP datagrams and dispatches incoming packets to the appropriate TCP socket. Your task is to implement the complete packet serialization/deserialization subsystem and the full TCP protocol engine so that all tests pass.

tasks.title: TJU Toy Compiler (Rust-like → RV32IM)
tasks.summary: A modern C++26 compiler for a Rust-like language targeting RV32IM assembly. The pipeline is: preprocessor → lexer → parser → semantic analysis → IR generation (quadruples) → code generation. Lexer, parser, AST, type system, symbol table, and compiler driver are pre-implemented; the three subsystems that require implementation are semantic checker, IR builder, and the four-file RISC-V code-generation backend.
tasks.instructionPreview: This project is a Rust-like language compiler from an undergraduate compilers sequence, implemented in modern C++ (C++26), that targets RV32IM assembly. The compiler pipeline consists of: preprocessing, lexing, parsing, semantic analysis, IR (quadruple) generation, and RISC-V assembly code generation. The project is already present in `/workspace` and builds with `make all`.

tasks.summary: A multi-lab OS course assignment in C and Python covering: file I/O with signal handling (Lab 0), a simple shell using fork/dup2/execvp (Lab 1A), multithreaded synchronization with a shared counter and a sorted linked list (Lab 2A), an ext2 filesystem image parser (Lab 3A), and a Python filesystem analyzer with a syntax fix (Lab 3B). tasks.instructionPreview: This is an undergraduate operating-systems lab assignment. The repository contains several labs covering C systems programming: file I/O, a simple shell, multithreaded synchronization, sorted linked lists, an ext2 filesystem parser, and a Python filesystem analyzer.

tasks.title: Z3 Fixedpoint Verification Tool Chain
tasks.summary: A chain of verification tools built on Z3's Python API. Starting with core SMT constraint solving (N-Queens, graph coloring, scheduling), through the μZ fixedpoint/Datalog engine for program analysis (reachability, Horn clause safety, reaching definitions), to the Spacer property-directed reachability engine for safety verification of transition systems (counter systems, Lamport's Bakery mutual exclusion), an imperative-to-Horn clause translator for program verification, and a CEGAR-style counterexample minimizer with predicate refinement. Each module builds incrementally on Z3's capabilities, forming a complete formal verification pipeline.
tasks.instructionPreview: Implement a chain of verification tools using Z3's Python API. The workspace contains a base framework with graph data structures (Graph, Petersen graph, Diamond DAG, Cyclic graph), transition system representations, and Horn clause benchmark files.
